bio.bio_blurb: Wife of Vermont Governor John Wolcott Stewart

narrative_blocks.header: She wed into her own Seymour family
narrative_blocks.body: Her mother was Emma Hart Seymour, daughter of Horatio Seymour — the same legal and political family in whose office her future husband would study law. She married the future governor in 1860 and bore five children.

narrative_blocks.header: She died in Boston, far from Vermont
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Cleveland and married into Middlebury’s leading family, she died in Boston in 1900, fifteen years before her husband — and was carried back to lie in the Middlebury ground he would join.

bio.bio_blurb: Wife of Vermont Governor John Wolcott Stewart

narrative_blocks.header: She wed into her own Seymour family
narrative_blocks.body: Her mother was Emma Hart Seymour, daughter of Horatio Seymour — the same legal and political family in whose office her future husband would study law. She married the future governor in 1860 and bore five children.

narrative_blocks.header: She died in Boston, far from Vermont
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Cleveland and married into Middlebury’s leading family, she died in Boston in 1900, fifteen years before her husband — and was carried back to lie in the Middlebury ground he would join.

narrative_blocks.header: First governor Vermont let serve two years
narrative_blocks.body: When Vermont changed its governor’s term from one year to two, he was the first man elected to the longer term, taking office in 1870 after years as Speaker of the state house.

narrative_blocks.header: He helped swing Vermont from Seward to Lincoln
narrative_blocks.body: As a delegate to the 1860 Republican national convention he played a part in moving the Vermont delegation from Seward to Lincoln — a small hinge in the nomination that made Lincoln president.

narrative_blocks.header: At eighty-two he filled a dead senator’s seat
narrative_blocks.body: When Senator Redfield Proctor died in 1908, the governor appointed the eighty-two-year-old Stewart to hold the seat until a successor was chosen — a capstone to a career begun before the Civil War.

narrative_blocks.header: He read law under the man he’d succeed
narrative_blocks.body: He studied in the office of Horatio Seymour, a future Vermont governor, and was admitted to the bar in 1850; his brother Dugald rose in the same years to state auditor.

narrative_blocks.header: He left Congress to run a bank
narrative_blocks.body: Declining renomination in 1890, he went home to run the Middlebury bank as its president — a quieter second career after decades in public life.

cross_connections.display_label: was his daughter-in-law, who married his son Philip

bio.bio_blurb: Colorado legislator and Roosevelt hunting companion

narrative_blocks.header: He camped in the wild with a president
narrative_blocks.body: In Colorado he became a hunting and camping companion of Theodore Roosevelt, close enough that the president twice tried to pull him into the federal government.

narrative_blocks.header: Roosevelt wanted him to run the land office
narrative_blocks.body: The president pressed him to become Commissioner of the General Land Office, promising he’d work beside Gifford Pinchot in a housecleaning of the Interior Department. Stewart was in no hurry — and Roosevelt invited him and his wife to stay at the White House.

narrative_blocks.header: A marble bust outlived the man
narrative_blocks.body: In 1884 the sculptor Charles Calverley cut his likeness in marble; a century later a museum paired the bust with images of anonymous women, asking why privileged men so dominate what we keep.

narrative_blocks.header: A governor’s son married a newspaper fortune
narrative_blocks.body: He married Sarah Frances Cowles, daughter of the Chicago Tribune’s Alfred Cowles, joining Vermont political royalty to a newspaper dynasty — and the couple made their home far from both, in Colorado Springs.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, the Vermont governor

narrative_blocks.header: Her gift to Yale became an economics powerhouse
narrative_blocks.body: With her two brothers she gave Yale $350,000 in 1928 to honor their father — a foundation that grew into the Cowles Foundation for Research in Economics, a force in the field to this day.

narrative_blocks.header: A newspaper fortune wed a governor’s son
narrative_blocks.body: Daughter of the Chicago Tribune’s Alfred Cowles, she married Philip Battell Stewart, a Vermont governor’s son, and built a home with him in Colorado Springs.

narrative_blocks.header: He left Vermont but returned to rest
narrative_blocks.body: He spent his life far from the family seat — born in Boston, dying in California at eighty — yet was carried back to the Middlebury ground where the Stewarts gather, generation after generation.

narrative_blocks.header: He married twice, thirty-six years apart
narrative_blocks.body: His first marriage came in 1920, his second not until 1956 — a span of thirty-six years between the two women who shared his long life.

burialCemetery.name: Middlebury Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Middlebury Cemetery, Middlebury Addison Co VT. Edward Payson Hooker family + David G. Hooker's first wife. Hooker tree: Sarah Parker Harris Hooker (X-prefix, David G's first wife, d.1868 age 30); Edward Clarendon Hooker (H03917, m. Ruth Fairchild); Emily Griswold Hooker (H03916).
